summary: Spolsky reviews Bruce Tate's 'Beyond Java' through the lens of Brooks' 'No Silver Bullet' essay, which distinguishes between accidental and essential complexity in programming. He traces five major advances in eliminating accidental difficulties (assemblers through memory-managed languages) and asks what the sixth will be. The book argues that experienced Java programmers are migrating to Python and Ruby, and Spolsky identifies explicit type declarations as the central accidental difficulty that these newer languages eliminate. He concludes that the book makes a compelling case worth reading, while maintaining his characteristic skepticism toward programming language hype.
key_points: Brooks' 'No Silver Bullet' distinguishes accidental difficulties (solvable by better tools) from essential complexity (inherent to software), predicting diminishing returns from new technologies

There have been only five major advances in eliminating accidental programming difficulties since the 1950s: assemblers, algebraic languages, structured languages, declarative languages, and memory-managed languages
The central thesis of 'Beyond Java' is that explicit type declarations are the key accidental difficulty driving experienced Java programmers toward Python and Ruby
Dynamic languages like Python, Ruby, and JavaScript solve real accidental difficulties rather than being mere hype
money_quotes: Programmers under the age of thirty probably don't realize why Aged Senile Programmers like me are so slow to adopt exciting new programming languages the day they come out.

Programming consists of overcoming two things: accidental difficulties, things which are difficult because you happen to be using inadequate programming tools, and things which are actually difficult, which no programming tool or language is going to solve.

Improvements in programming languages can eliminate accidental difficulties, but after you've done that, you're left with the actual complexity of software development, so the No Silver Bullet theory basically warns us to expect diminishing returns from new technologies.
To a historian, it's starting to look like type declarations are one of those accidental difficulties that good programming languages can eliminate.
An example of an accidental difficulty is manual memory management, e.g. 'malloc' and 'free,' or the singleton classes people create in Java because they don't have top level functions.
key_insight: Viewed through Brooks' essential vs. accidental complexity framework, explicit type declarations appear to be the next major accidental difficulty that programming languages can and should eliminate.
